pageTitle.value: Andrew Bastawrous' Peek Vision - Perpetual Planet

pageCaption.value: Through his social enterprise Peek Vision, Andrew Bastawrous has helped millions in remote communities access life-changing eye tests.

WaPageTitle.value: Andrew Bastawrous' Peek Vision - Perpetual Planet

metadescription.value: Through his social enterprise Peek Vision, Andrew Bastawrous has helped millions in remote communities access life-changing eye tests. More on rolex.org.

text.value: Ophthalmologist and Rolex Awards Laureate Andrew Bastawrous founded Peek Vision to bring eye care to remote communities through smartphone technology and public health tools. With support from the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative, his social enterprise has enabled many millions of people to access eye tests that are readily available in the developed world.

title.value: AIMING TO BRING EYE TESTS TO ALL

text.value: Andrew Bastawrous’ Peek Vision has recently celebrated a major milestone: 10 million people have been screened for sight issues across 12 countries. Every day, around the world, thousands of health-care providers visit local communities and deliver specially developed app-based eye tests. This incredible achievement began with a simple ambition: to get tests to those who would otherwise not have access to them. The Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative has supported Bastawrous in his journey to turn his goal into reality.

After spending time as an eye surgeon in the UK’s National Health Service, Bastawrous reached a crossroad in his career. Rather than become a consultant, he realized he wanted to get essential eye care to those living in remote places. “There are more than half a billion people today who are vision impaired and could be helped with a simple pair of glasses,” says Bastawrous. As part of a PhD with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, he moved to Kenya with his family to set up 100 temporary eye clinics. While successful, it became clear that this model was not enough. Instead of bringing people to the specialists, to truly have an impact, Bastawrous needed to get specialists out to where the people are. “To be able to move that service all the way to the level of their home could be transformational,” he says.

title.value: “There are more than half a billion people today who are vision impaired and could be helped with a simple pair of glasses.”
EQUALITY AND ACCESSIBILITY IN EYE CARE

text.value: From this realization, Peek Vision was born, but there were still many challenges to overcome. As communities lacked roads and medical services, eye tests had to be conducted by non-experts, easy to produce on a large scale and distribute, and require very few additional resources. From his time in Kenya, Bastawrous knew that one thing was becoming increasingly common: good internet coverage and access to smartphones. Peek Vision began developing mobile phone-based technologies for identifying vision conditions, and software that could connect people with services and track their onward journeys.

The company took off, and with the support of the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative, Bastawrous and his team expanded from Kenya into multiple locations across Africa. By 2019, they were screening 64,000 people a year.

“I remember saying, ‘We need to be reaching that number of people, not every year, but every week,’” says Bastawrous, a 2016 Rolex Awards Laureate. Continuing to improve and expand Peek Vision, he and his team saw a meteoric rise in the use of their services in Asia. In India, Peek Vision partners with Dr Shroff’s Charity Eye Hospital, which is recognized globally for its eye care. Founded in 1922 with a goal of eradicating avoidable blindness in all sections of society, it is one of the oldest and biggest eye care institutions in India. Peek Vision has been collaborating with the hospital on the many large eye-care programmes the institution runs in northern India through three research and development projects that make use of cutting-edge software and public health tools. Bastawrous says: “Looking at where we are now, up to 120,000 people are being screened every week in over 80 programmes across 12 countries.”

Bastawrous’ aspirations show no sign of diminishing. “Now there’s a responsibility to take what’s possible and deliver it at another level,” he says, “That’s where I’m focused now. What we’ve done so far is a good start, but it is only the start.” Peek Vision plans to reach the many millions more who have treatable eye conditions and may not even realize they would benefit from basic care.

It might seem surprising that someone would not know they needed glasses, but Bastawrous himself can attest to the experience. After struggling in school and being labelled clumsy, at age 12 he had an eye test that transformed his world. Up until that point, he did not know his vision was impaired. Then, with just a pair of glasses, his life changed. Suddenly, the blurs on the whiteboard at school were clear and he could catch up with his classmates. That same year, Bastawrous travelled with his parents to their home country of Egypt where he noticed other short-sighted children were not getting the tests or treatment he had been so impacted by. Bastawrous keenly felt the injustice that access to eye care was dependent on where you were born or lived, and the desire to right this wrong has stayed with him since.

These two early lessons continue to guide his efforts. On a recent trip to northern India, he met a 14-year-old boy called Prem. Like Bastawrous, he had been struggling with poor academic performance due to untreated vision problems. Eventually, this led Prem to drop out of school. However, after receiving glasses through a Peek Vision-powered Community Eye Health screening programme, he was able to return to school and even started playing his favourite sport again – cricket.

title.value: “Now there’s a responsibility to take what’s possible and deliver it at another level.”

text.value: The Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative supports Peek Vision in sharing stories like that of Prem and raising awareness of how science and technology are being used for the betterment of humanity. Bastawrous is committed to the belief that “better never stops”. As he puts it, “You can always get better and there will always be problems, but we can overcome them.”

Across the globe, every day, thousands will begin losing their sight. For Bastawrous, that does not mean more people need to be living with vision loss. With the support of the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative, he plans to ensure that everyone has the chance to get the eye care that allows them to live life to the fullest, no matter their circumstances. As Bastawrous proves, a simple pair of glasses can change the world.

pageTitle.value: Mark Kendall - Medical alerts from a microwearable sticker - Rolex.org

pageCaption.value: From creating a pain-free vaccine patch, Australian scientist Mark Kendall is now developing microwearable devices that send early warnings when patients are experiencing events like heart attacks.

WaPageTitle.value: Mark Kendall - Medical alerts from a microwearable sticker - Rolex.org

metadescription.value: Scientist and 2012 Rolex Laureate Mark Kendall has produced microwearable sensors that provide critical medical information. Learn more on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Medical alerts from a sticker on your hand

pageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Taking a volcano’s pulse

pageCaption.value: Eight hundred million people live in the shadow of fiery death, within striking range of one of Earth’s 500 historically active volcanoes. For Scottish volcanologist Andrew McGonigle, providing timely warning of an impending eruption is a goal that is both humanitarian and scientific.

WaPageTitle.value: Taking a volcano’s pulse - Rolex Awards

metadescription.value: Using drone technology to predict volcanic eruptions, Andrew McGonigle’s work is both humanitarian and scientific. Learn more on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Listening to the Earth

pageCaption.value: Since designing an underwater system to prevent ships from colliding with whales, bioacoustics pioneer Michel André has expanded his project to create a network of microphones that monitor the planet's myriad sounds and warn of threats to nature.

WaPageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Listening to the Earth

metadescription.value: Michel André’s bioacoustics work has led to a growing network of underwater microphones that aims to protect marine life. Learn more on rolex.org.
